planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, average to sandy soil in full sun; downy gentian grows naturally in dry to average prairies, open oak savannas, and barrens of the upper Midwest where it blooms very late in fall — often the last native wildflower of the season to bloom.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average soil in full sun; downy gentian is a native prairie gentian of the upper Midwest, producing clusters of brilliant, deep blue-violet, vase-shaped flowers at the tips of upright stems in September–October — one of the last native wildflowers to bloom in the prairie season. The closed, finely-pubescent (downy-hairy) flower buds give the species its common name. Like all prairie gentians, the flowers open only in full sunlight and close at night or on cloudy days. A valuable late-season pollinator plant, providing nectar for bumblebees, monarch butterflies, and metallic sweat bees in the final weeks before fall frosts. Best in lean, dry prairie conditions — excess fertility causes lax, floppy growth. Combine with grasses, blazingstar, and other late-blooming prairie species for an authentic fall prairie display. Long-lived in appropriate dry, open prairie conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in partial shade to full sun; stiff gentian grows naturally in moist, open woodlands, woodland edges, and roadsides of the eastern US and Appalachian Mountains, blooming in late summer and fall after two years of growth as a biennial.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in partial shade to full sun; stiff gentian (agueweed) is a native biennial of the eastern US, producing stiffly upright, branched stems with clusters of small, pale violet to blue-violet, tubular flowers at the stem tips in late summer through fall. Less showy than the fringed or bottle gentians, stiff gentian compensates with its prolific branching and production of many smaller flowers. The common name 'agueweed' comes from a traditional use — the bitter roots were used medicinally for fevers (ague) by eastern Native Americans and early settlers. The bitter principle (gentiopicrin) shared by all gentians has been used in digestive bitters. Biennial, reliably self-seeding in moist, partially shaded woodland garden edges and disturbed areas to maintain persistent colonies. A useful, relatively easy native for woodland garden edges and naturalistic settings.

planting.note: Plant small corms in fall 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; western spring beauty is a true spring ephemeral that grows from a small, deep corm and disappears completely by early summer. It grows naturally in mountain meadows and open forests as snow melts.

directions: Plant corms in fall 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade; western spring beauty is the Rocky Mountain and Pacific Northwest counterpart of the familiar eastern spring beauty (Claytonia virginica), producing lanceolate rather than grasslike leaves and charming clusters of white to pale pink flowers with darker pink veins in early spring as snow melts. A small, delicate spring ephemeral growing from a deep, starchy corm (called bitterroot or Indian potato) that was an important food for mountain Native Americans and early explorers. Bears ate the newly emerged flowering plants after emerging from winter dens. Naturalizes in appropriate conditions, spreading by corms and self-sowing. Goes completely dormant by early summer. Perfect for planting under deciduous trees or shrubs where it will receive ample spring light but summer shade will not be a problem, as the plant is long gone.

planting.note: Plant divisions or fresh seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; false rue anemone is a delicate spring ephemeral of moist, rich deciduous forest floors in the eastern and central US, naturalized by spreading tuberous roots into modest patches.

directions: Plant fresh seeds or small tuber divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in full shade to partial shade; false rue anemone is a delicate native spring ephemeral of rich, moist deciduous forest floors across the eastern and central US, producing small, compound leaves very similar to rue anemone (Thalictrum thalictroides) — the two species are similar enough that false rue anemone was long classified with Isopyrum. The small, white, five-petaled, star-shaped flowers are produced in early spring in great profusion on the small, branched plants. Despite the modest size, a naturalized colony provides a lovely, delicate spring display in deep shade where few other plants bloom. The plant is gone by late spring. Spreads slowly by tuberous roots and self-sowing. Easy to overlook if not in flower — pairs well with trilliums, wild ginger, and other spring wildflowers for a rich woodland spring garden composition.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, dry, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun; prickly phlox is a native California chaparral shrub adapted to hot, dry, rocky slopes and sandy coastal scrub, tolerating drought and heat once established. Avoid excess water or clay soil.

directions: Plant cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; prickly phlox is a native California shrub of chaparral, coastal sage scrub, and dry rocky slopes in the California Ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills, producing densely needle-like, prickly foliage (much sharper and more aggressive than any true phlox) and profuse, bright rose-pink, five-petaled flowers that closely resemble garden phlox in spring. The combination of phlox-like flowers and needle-prickle foliage is unmistakable. Despite belonging to the same family (Polemoniaceae) as true phlox, it is in a different genus. A spectacular, California-native flowering shrub for dry, hot, well-drained gardens in zones 8–10, producing one of the showiest spring flower displays of any California native shrub. Excellent for slope stabilization, xeriscape, and fire-resistant California native gardens. Hummingbirds and native bees visit the flowers.
